design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore interconnected mansion, solve environmental puzzles using character-specific items, manage limited resources (health tonics, weapons), encounter random combat encounters, make irreversible character survival decisions
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited healing items) combined with permanent character death mechanics; players must decide which characters to risk and whether to engage combat or flee
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a documentary film crew through a haunted mansion, uncovering the dark history of its inhabitants while solving environmental puzzles and surviving supernatural threats
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Uncompromising commitment to permanent consequences: dead characters stay dead and cannot be revived, forcing players to adapt strategy. Five different endings based on survivor count. Heavy emphasis on environmental storytelling through scattered diary entries and notes rather than exposition. Puzzle design requires backtracking with newly acquired items (Metroidvania-style), creating cognitive satisfaction from spatial mastery.

difficulty.lives_system: No traditional lives system. Characters can permanently die; when a character dies, their unique item function is replaced by substitute items found near their corpse
difficulty.checkpointing: No explicit checkpoint system documented; progression continues regardless of character survival, enabling multiple playstyles
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through character management decisions. Players choose which characters to deploy, whether to engage or flee from combat, inventory distribution across party members, and risk/reward calculations for puzzle attempts. Quick-time events add reflexive difficulty during trap encounters

progression.upgrade_system: No traditional RPG leveling. Progression through acquisition of character-specific tools (camera, lighter, medical kit, lockpick, vacuum cleaner) that unlock new areas and puzzle solutions. Additional consumable items (tonics for healing) scattered throughout mansion
progression.unlock_mechanism: Environmental gating through puzzle requirements. Specific character items required to progress (e.g., lockpick to open doors). Backtracking required once new items acquired
replayability_hooks: Five different endings based on number of survivors at final boss
Different character party compositions create varying tactical approaches
Puzzle solutions may vary depending on which characters survive and which substitute items are available
Optional environmental storytelling (notes, diary entries) discoverable through exploration

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through scattered diary entries and secret messages
Visual storytelling via projection room sequence revealing ghost's tragic backstory (couple and baby burning)
Mansion layout itself as narrative device—exploration reveals history of the space

boss_design.philosophy: Final boss represents narrative resolution rather than mechanical gauntlet. Lady Mamiya confrontation serves as climax to emotional story arc established through environmental clues. Boss encounter reflects game's emphasis on narrative consequence over mechanical complexity
notable_bosses: Lady Mamiya (final boss): Ghost of mansion's former mistress, antagonist revealed through environmental storytelling and projection room sequence. Confrontation reflects players' team composition and resource management throughout game

faction_indicators: Documentary film crew identity creates in-group cohesion
Five distinct characters with specialized roles (camera operator, medic, etc.) suggest individual skill specializations
Character survival creates persistent in-game faction status
danger_signals: Permanent character death mechanics signal high-stakes consequences
Resource scarcity (limited tonics) creates desperation pressure
Mansion collapse imminent—environmental danger escalates urgency
Random combat encounters create unpredictable threat distribution
Quick-time events punish inattention with instant death
environmental_storytelling: Abandoned mansion in state of decay signals prior tragedy
Projection room revealing child death via imagery creates emotional weight
Scattered diary entries chronicle previous victims and failed escape attempts
Character corpses found with substitute items emphasize permanent consequences
Incinerator location tied to tragic backstory—spatial narrative reinforcement
